message:
feat(connectors): declare granular optional capabilities across 81 manifests

Expand the `[capabilities] optional = [...]` block on every connector
manifest except bigquery (which was tightened in a dedicated commit)
so that capability tokens can be issued against narrow, per-operation
capabilities instead of always carrying the coarse "<connector>.*"
grant. The capabilities listed here are the superset of operations the
connector exposes in its `[provides.operations.*]` table, split into
the natural verbs for that surface (read / write / delete / search /
webhooks.manage / items.* / etc).

Examples of the patterns applied:

  1password        -> 1password.items.{read,write}, 1password.vaults.read
  airtable         -> airtable.{read,write,delete,webhooks.manage}
  algolia          -> algolia.{indices,records,search}.{read,write}
  amplitude        -> amplitude.{charts,cohorts,events}.read
  anthropic        -> anthropic.{chat,get_usage,message,message.stream}
  arxiv            -> arxiv.{read,search,citations,authors,categories,monitor}
  asana            -> asana.{projects,tasks,users,teams,workspaces}.{read,write}
  bitbucket        -> bitbucket.{repos,prs,branches,commits}.{read,write}
  clickup, cron, datadog, discord, dropbox, elasticsearch, figma,
  gcp, gitlab, gmail, grafana, homeassistant, hubspot, intercom,
  jira, kubernetes, linkedin, mailchimp, mcp-bridge, metabase,
  microsoft365, mongodb, n8n, notion, openai, pinecone, plaid,
  postgresql, posthog, qdrant, reddit, redis, retool, roam, s3,
  salesforce, segment, semanticscholar, sendgrid, sentry, slack,
  snowflake, spotify, stripe, telegram, terraform, todoist, trello,
  twilio, twitter, vectordb, webhook-receiver, whisper, zapier,
  zendesk — each gets its own narrowed capability set following the
  same verb-per-resource shape.

What this unblocks:

- Hosts can now mint tokens that name e.g. `salesforce.leads.read`
  instead of `salesforce.*`, so a compromised or overly-eager caller
  cannot silently escalate to `salesforce.leads.delete`.
- Policy review is easier because the allowlist is materialized in
  the manifest rather than inferred from the operations table.
- Matches the granular compliance-suite expectations in the updated
  crates/fcp-e2e/tests/*_compliance_e2e.rs files (committed separately).

Additive only: no operations moved between required/optional/forbidden,
no `host_allow`/`port_allow`/`ip_allow` changes, no new network
permissions. The `required` / `forbidden` lists and every
`[provides.operations.*.network_constraints]` block are unchanged
modulo whitespace.

Also drops one `*.googleapis.com` entry left over in a non-bigquery
manifest where it was dead (host_allow trimmed to the single
googleapis subdomain the connector actually calls).

files.patch:
@@ -28,7 +28,12 @@ forbidden = ["z:public", "z:community", "z:work"]
 
 [capabilities]
 required = ["network.dns", "network.egress", "network.tls.sni", "storage.state"]
-optional = []
+optional = [
+    "terraform.plan",
+    "terraform.apply",
+    "terraform.state",
+    "terraform.state_write",
+]
 forbidden = ["system.exec", "network.listen", "media.download", "media.upload"]
 
 # ── Read-only plan operations ───────────────────────────────────────
